title: TI-722511
description: Support incident opened 27.03.24 (Ticket name: KF11: Management Report not working, neither in UAT nor in PROD). Created via Internet; assigned owner Employee 13. Records multiple portal notes, follow-up activities and a closure on 11.04.24 with closure text: "Best-practice cipher suites activated with IISCRYPTO and AOS servers restarted". The ticket documents reproducible errors when accessing F&O → General Ledger → Financial Reports → Edit across environments and users, investigation steps, communications with Avanade, and references to an internal JIRA ticket reporting the same error.

title: CUSTOMER COMPANY 11
description: CUSTOMER COMPANY 11 is the customer organization and tenant context for which the "Management Reporting & Financial Reporting Infrastructure Standard" was written; the document describes the D365 Finance & Operations financial reporting infrastructure that operates in Customer Company 11's UAT and PROD environments. Support tickets TI-722423 and TI-722511 were opened on behalf of CUSTOMER COMPANY 11 (TI-722423 references Contact Person 8 and Contact Person 2); the incident described in TI-722511 affects EMEA finance users across the same UAT and PROD tenant environments.

title: CONTACT PERSON 7
description: Named contact for the ticket who reported or is the primary customer contact for the incident.

title: EMPLOYEE 13
description: EMPLOYEE 13 is the support engineer and ticket owner who acted as the incident investigator and coordinator. They recreated the FRDB in the D365FO_INTE environment and executed the Data Mart Reset as remediation steps, opened a Microsoft support ticket (#2404050050000982), and are listed as the owner of TI-722511. EMPLOYEE 13 authored portal notes and posted status updates about scheduling and progress while coordinating updates and communications with stakeholders and external support.

title: EMPLOYEE 14
description:
EMPLOYEE 14 served as the contact person and primary reporter for the incident involving connectivity problems with the TRAIN CE server, submitting the initial message and requesting the ticket be reopened when the issue recurred on 09.12.24. The ticket was assigned to EMPLOYEE 14 on 26.11.24; they therefore functioned both as the assigned support/operations contact and as a participant in the ticket workflow, receiving assignment actions and acting as point of contact throughout the ticket lifecycle.  

As support staff, EMPLOYEE 14 added portal notes (portal messages show a NO‑REPLY address employee14@consultingcompany.ch), forwarded the case to a system engineer, and participated in calls and triage with Artur, Ms. X and Avanade. They reported the error reproduced across all environments, users and machines, and investigated Report Designer performance—observing it was extremely slow except when accessed directly via 001‑srv‑aos72. EMPLOYEE 14 logged the next remediation step as a Data Mart Reset and recommended opening a Microsoft support ticket if the reset did not resolve the issue.

title: MICROSOFT CRM PORTALS
description: MICROSOFT CRM PORTALS is a customer‑facing CRM portal used to open support tickets, upload and download file attachments (including PDFs and screenshots), and post incident activity. Ticket history entries are labeled like "Microsoft CRM Portals, #" and show that the portal created tickets, generated system notes (several "New portal note" entries), and attached files referenced in ticket activity. The portal acts as a logging and communication channel between external users and internal teams (including references to an internal Mexico ticket), capturing user‑reported symptoms, screenshots, status updates, and portal‑generated status messages (for example entries showing an issue marked resolved then later reporting sensors unhealthy again), and serves as the source of the portal’s incident history and status messages.

title: BU CUSTOMER SERVICES RUN
description: BU CUSTOMER SERVICES RUN is an internal business unit/team at Consulting Sample Company responsible for run‑time and customer‑service operations. Tickets routed to "BU Customer Services Run" (often noted in ticket notes as the assignment target and sometimes appearing as the no‑reply sender BU_CustomerServices_RUN) become the operational owner during the CRM workflow—typically the first assignee after Admin/CRM—and receive automated notifications. The unit operates an internal mailbox/team that handles ongoing incident response and operational requests (for example, it was assigned to manage a customer request about the FileZilla Server on the virtual server KF14).

title: SYSTEM ENGINEER
description: SYSTEM ENGINEER — The role/team that received the forwarded ticket TI-1385257 (forwarded by Employee 16) and was designated as the responsible technical assignee/escalation during initial triage. The System Engineer is accountable for reproducing and investigating the error across environments and users, performing technical remediation and system-level changes, and resolving the incident. Actions attributed to this role include repair installations and configuration checks/updates on the CE Train server and ADFS, with repair work recorded on 02.12.24.

title: CRM DEVELOPMENT
description: CRM DEVELOPMENT is the CRM sub-team responsible for application-level development, automation and diagnostics within the organization’s ticketing/CRM system. The team appears in portal and log activity as "ApplicationUser, CRM Development," indicating actions recorded under a service or application account that represent the team’s automated or manual interactions with workflows. CRM DEVELOPMENT authors status checks and scheduling notes, implements development/automation features, and performs diagnostics and application changes that advance or pause tickets and workflow steps. Log entries sometimes show the team in a "Waiting" state, suggesting they are either awaiting input or are the party responsible for the next action or status update in the workflow. In summary, CRM DEVELOPMENT functions as the technical owner for CRM application changes, automation logic and operational diagnostics tied to the ticketing/CRM lifecycle.

title: APPLICATIONUSER
description:
APPLICATIONUSER is an automated/service account (a non‑human, process-level identity) used by the CRM and web-portal systems. Logs and portal activity reference it as "ApplicationUser, CRM Development | WEB" and "ApplicationUser, CRM Development," and it is observed performing automated actions such as classifying ticket priority and other CRM/portal workflows. The account is therefore associated with the CRM Development function and is used programmatically by the CRM/WEB stack to make or record system changes.

Inferred operational and governance notes: APPLICATIONUSER should be treated as a managed service account (documented owner: CRM Development), scoped with least privilege for the specific ticketing and portal operations it performs, and instrumented with dedicated logging/alerting. Credentials or secrets for APPLICATIONUSER should be rotated and stored in a secrets manager (or replaced by a platform-managed identity/service principal where possible), and its use controlled by change/runbook processes and regular access reviews to reduce abuse or drift.

title: AVANADE
description: AVANADE is an external third-party vendor/partner engaged in the investigation and troubleshooting effort. AVANADE participated in a troubleshooting call alongside internal support staff and customer representatives, collaborating with both parties during the investigation.

title: REGINA JAGSCH
description: Customer contact from Austria who reported that none of the EMEA finance employees could open a report and observed that creators of old reports appeared as "unknown" — provided symptom detail indicating a global impact.

title: ARTUR
description: ARTUR participated as a stakeholder in a troubleshooting call alongside Employee 14, Ms. X, and Avanade representatives. He was actively involved in the joint investigation and investigative communications to identify and reproduce an error that manifested across multiple environments and machines.

title: MS. X
description: MS. X is the named customer/tester (anonymized in the source material) referenced in portal notes as a stakeholder and primary test contact responsible for validating fixes and performing acceptance testing. She was unavailable for testing and absent until 16.04.2024 (16 April 2024) and is expected to participate in validation and testing after her return.

title: DYNAMICS 365 F&O
description: The application platform (Finance & Operations / F&O) where users navigate to General Ledger → Financial Reports → Edit and encounter the intermittent error; the Management Report functionality is part of this service.

title: MANAGEMENT REPORT / FINANCIAL REPORTS
description: The specific F&O feature (Management/Financial Reports editing) that fails intermittently for users across environments; screenshots attached to portal notes show the error in this component.

title: DATA MART
description: DATA MART: The Data Mart is the reporting data store/aggregation layer (the reporting backend) used by reporting components. When reporting functionality or consistency problems occurred, a "Data Mart Reset" was identified as the next remediation step—planned and/or executed—to attempt to resolve the issue if initial remediation actions did not improve the situation and to restore report functionality and consistency.

title: JIRA ICO261-183
description: Internal/related JIRA ticket (https://jira.KF11ohm.com/browse/ICO261-183) opened by another internal team (Mexico) reporting the same error, indicating the problem is global and not isolated to one region.

title: IISCRYPTO
description: Tool/utility used to harden/modify TLS cipher suite configuration on Windows/IIS servers. Closure notes state best-practice cipher suites were activated using IISCRYPTO as part of the remediation.

title: AOS SERVERS
type: SERVER INFRASTRUCTURE (APPLICATION SERVERS, DOMAIN CONTROLLERS, VIRTUAL MACHINES)
description: AOS SERVERS (Application Object Server / F&O application servers) are the Application Object Server instances that broker authentication, communication and request traffic between clients (Report Designer and browser) and backend services such as the Financial Reporting Service and the Data Mart. They are essential to overall service availability and performance and must be restarted to apply TLS/Cipher Suite adjustments or similar configuration changes. During the referenced incident remediation, the AOS SERVERS were restarted and that restart was documented as part of the closure activity that resolved the incident.

title: BEST-PRACTICE CIPHER SUITES
description: Security configuration change applied to servers: activation of recommended/“best-practice” TLS cipher suites (performed with IISCRYPTO) as part of the remediation that, together with server restarts, resolved the issue.

title: ADFS
type: IDENTITY PROVIDER / AUTHENTICATION SERVICE (ADFS, ENTRA ID)
description: ADFS (Active Directory Federation Services) is the identity/federation provider for the Train integration. During the incident triage ADFS was initially considered then effectively excluded because only PROD was modified over the weekend and the error reproduced in all environments; however further investigation identified a misconfigured new ADFS client as the root cause. The client registration parameters EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders were set incorrectly, causing unexpected JWT issuance and forwarding of MFA claims and producing authentication failures; those parameters were adjusted to match the TEST environment, which resolved the failures. Recorded remediation actions included federation data updates in ADFS, removal of old certificates, and verification/checking of ADFS configuration.

title: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE
type: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE (FIREWALLS, VLANS, NAT, LOAD BALANCERS)
description: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE: During troubleshooting the network layer was explicitly examined; investigators performed network checks and reported, "No irregularities were found in the network." As a result, the network infrastructure was inspected and excluded as the root cause of the incident.

title: MICROSOFT
description: MICROSOFT — Microsoft Corporation is identified as the cloud and application vendor providing Teams, Microsoft 365 services and Microsoft CRM Portals for the environment. Diagnostics reference Microsoft explicitly (for example “Microsoft IP” seen in RTP media streams and checks against Microsoft 365 service status), indicating legitimate service traffic and reliance on Microsoft service health for troubleshooting. At the same time Microsoft’s branding has been spoofed by phishing/fake login pages, so Microsoft appears both as the legitimate provider and as the brand being impersonated. The customer has requested that any access restrictions preserve genuine Microsoft sign‑ins (do not block Microsoft service sign‑in flows). If local remediation (for example a Data Mart Reset) fails, Microsoft is the party to whom a support escalation would be raised (i.e., “open a Microsoft ticket”) for deeper platform/component support.

title: CONSULTING COMPANY
description: Support/consulting organization (implied by NO-REPLY portal emails like employee14@consultingcompany.ch) performing triage, coordinating with customer and third parties and posting portal updates.

title: MICROSOFT DYNAMICS CRM
description: The Dynamics CRM platform behind the Microsoft CRM Portals used to create and manage the portal records and attachments for the ticket lifecycle; CRM Development team and ApplicationUser context interact with this CRM environment.

title: IIS SERVERS
type: SERVER INFRASTRUCTURE (APPLICATION SERVERS, DOMAIN CONTROLLERS, VIRTUAL MACHINES)
description: Internet Information Services (IIS) web servers in the application landscape where TLS/cipher suite configuration was changed using IISCRYPTO as part of the remediation; part of the web/application server tier alongside AOS servers.

title: PORTAL NO-REPLY EMAIL (EMPLOYEE14@CONSULTINGCOMPANY.CH / EMPLOYEE13@CONSULTINGCOMPANY.CH)
description: The portal notification/from addresses (NO-REPLY) used by the Microsoft CRM Portals to post portal notes and automated emails (examples: employee14@consultingcompany.ch, employee13@consultingcompany.ch); represents the SMTP/email channel used by the ticketing portal.

title: JIRA (JIRA.KF11OHM.COM)
description: The JIRA instance used internally (referenced via https://jira.KF11ohm.com/browse/ICO261-183) to track related/internal issues (ICO261-183) reporting the same error from another region; a collaboration/issue-tracking system integrated into the incident workflow.

title: APPLICATIONUSER CRM DEVELOPMENT
description: System or role entry representing the CRM Development application user who posted status updates asking whether the Data Mart Reset produced improvement; appears as an author of log entries.

title: ADMIN CRM
description: Admin role/person in the CRM team who reassigned the ticket (noted as "Admin, CRM" in the timeline).

title: REGINA
description: End-user contact (from Austria) reporting that EMEA finance employees were unable to open reports and that previously created report authors were displayed as "unknown"; provided screenshots and status updates used in troubleshooting.

title: EMEA FINANCE
description: Business/user group affected by the incident (EMEA finance employees reported they could no longer open reports across the region).

title: REPORT DESIGNER
description:
REPORT DESIGNER is the client/editor used to open, edit and run financial reports hosted by the Financial Reporting Service. It has been observed to be extremely slow for both internal and external users except when launched directly on 001-srv-aos72, where performance is acceptable. Users also encounter "Report cannot be found" errors and missing author metadata (authors displayed as "unknown"). Startup performance and the ability to locate report authors/metadata are treated as key quality metrics for REPORT DESIGNER.

REPORT DESIGNER’s correct operation depends on TLS compatibility, service account authentication, and reliable connectivity to backend services—specifically AOS, the Data Mart, and SQL. The symptom pattern (fast only on 001-srv-aos72, global slowness otherwise, and missing author metadata) points to problems in one or more of those dependencies: TLS handshake or protocol mismatches between clients and services, service account authentication/permission failures that prevent author/metadata lookup, or network/DNS/firewall issues preventing clients from reaching AOS/Data Mart/SQL. The local fast-path on 001-srv-aos72 suggests that the server either has direct/back-channel access or cached metadata that remote clients lack.

title: 001-SRV-AOS72
type: SERVER INFRASTRUCTURE (APPLICATION SERVERS, DOMAIN CONTROLLERS, VIRTUAL MACHINES)
description: Application server/VM that, when accessed directly, allowed the Report Designer to function properly (indicates the service is reachable/performant on this node). Used as a diagnostic reference point where reports worked.

title: 001-SRV-AOS73
type: SERVER INFRASTRUCTURE (APPLICATION SERVERS, DOMAIN CONTROLLERS, VIRTUAL MACHINES)
description: Sibling application server/VM in the same tier that was observed to respond very sluggishly during investigations (implicated in degraded Report Designer performance).

title: FRDB IN D365FO_INTE
type: SERVER INFRASTRUCTURE (APPLICATION SERVERS, DOMAIN CONTROLLERS, VIRTUAL MACHINES)
description: FRDB database instance (or database schema) within the D365FO_INTE environment that was recreated by the assigned engineer as part of remediation steps; tied to reporting/data availability for reports.

title: D365FO_INTE
description: Dynamics 365 Finance & Operations integration/test environment (named D365FO_INTE) where FRDB was recreated and where the Data Mart/FRDB artifacts are managed; part of the environments impacted or used for remediation.

title: JIRA.KF11.COM
description: Internal ticketing/JIRA system referenced in notes (link included). Used to track related internal tickets (example: an internal ticket from Mexico referencing the same/global issue).

title: MICROSOFT SUPPORT TICKET #2404050050000982
description: External Microsoft support case opened on 05.04.24 (ID #2404050050000982) to escalate the global reporting issue. Created by internal staff (Employee 13) after preliminary troubleshooting and Data Mart operations were performed.

title: TI-1623017
description: Support incident opened 18.11.25 (Ticket name: KF60: Incorrect printer automatically set as default). Company: Customer Company 60 AG. Contact: Contact Person 18. Owner: Employee 23. Origin: Email. Issue: Default printer for several Finance department users automatically switched to "MFP-Zuerich-01" despite users being at the Basel location. Ticket includes remote troubleshooting, GPO analysis, test changes, coordination with customer, and closure on 24.11.25 with note that printer assignment and default configuration were corrected according to the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard; behavior stabilized.

title: CUSTOMER COMPANY 60 AG
description: CUSTOMER COMPANY 60 AG is the corporate customer reporting multiple, related printing and Scan-to-Mail incidents across its Basel and Zurich sites. The organization opened a support ticket (reported via Contact Person 28 and Contact Person 04) and consulting support performed tests and verification on its behalf. Symptoms include failed deliveries of larger scanned documents sent via Scan-to-Mail (notably from a Zurich multifunction device), employees in the Basel office being unable to print to the affected Basel printer despite colleagues being able to see the device on the local site/network, and multiple Finance department users in Basel experiencing their default printer automatically switching to a Zurich device. The affected printers and users are part of CUSTOMER COMPANY 60 AG’s environment. The customer reports recent changes to network/DNS and external mail security settings (including new DNS forwarding), which were made before the incidents and may be relevant to the Scan-to-Mail delivery failures and to device discovery/routing behavior. The incident was logged and is being handled for CUSTOMER COMPANY 60 AG with ongoing support engagement.

title: CONTACT PERSON 18
description: CONTACT PERSON 18 is the named liaison at both Customer Company 52 AG and Customer Company 60 AG who reported the incident and coordinated follow-up. They initiated the report (email 12.08.25), supplied additional context noting a recent tightening of Microsoft Teams security policies, communicated details about affected users, and served as the recipient for ticket updates and confirmations of testing outcomes.

title: EMPLOYEE 23
description: EMPLOYEE 23 is recorded as both the CRM/admin user who opened the ticket on 18.07.24 (creation recorded in the CRM/WEB) and the support engineer assigned as the ticket owner and primary early responder. EMPLOYEE 23 initiated a remote connection and performed the initial analysis and tests (including temporary removal of an incorrect Active Directory group membership, gpupdate /force, and user re‑login), restarted the print spooler when printing from SAP via VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 failed, and activated the infrastructure on‑call. They coordinated remediation and restore actions with the customer and shift teams, applied corrections according to the blueprint, validated the outcome with multiple users, recorded multiple WEB log entries documenting these activities, communicated follow‑up to shifts and the customer contact, and subsequently closed the ticket.

title: CONSULTING SAMPLE COMPANY
description: CONSULTING SAMPLE COMPANY is the external consulting/service-provider that owns the support portal (including NO-REPLY notifications and addresses such as Mitarbeiter15@ConsultingSampleCompany.ch), appears on ticket recipients and closure signatures, and acts as the primary point of contact for incidents affecting customer organizations (e.g., Customer Company 12, Customer Company 60 AG). It is responsible for end-to-end incident handling: triage, systems-engineering troubleshooting, coordinating and escalating with internal teams (mail, architecture), executing remediation actions guided by its internal blueprints and standards (for example the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and Direct Routing architecture/blueprint), applying configuration changes (scan profiles, SMTP adjustments, SMTP relay and DNS updates, selective SMTP AUTH enablement), making Conditional Access and authentication fixes (policy changes, re-authentication, device sync, token invalidation), running Azure AD Connect synchronizations, performing queue activation/restarts and remote checks, adjusting sensitivity labels and permission groups, testing fixes in test tenants, validating functionality (e.g., Scan-to-Mail, mailflow, access stabilization), monitoring results, updating the portal, and issuing closure communications once resolution is confirmed.

title: MFP-ZUERICH-01
description: Network/shared multifunction printer located/assigned to the Zurich location. It was incorrectly applied as the default printer for affected Finance users in Basel due to AD group/GPO mapping.

title: MFP-BASEL-01
description:
MFP-BASEL-01 (printer queue name "MFP-Basel-01") is the network/shared multifunction printer assigned to the Basel location. It was intended to be the default printer for the affected user cohort once the group/GPO deployment was corrected. Users targeting the MFP-Basel-01 queue experienced two related sets of symptoms: print jobs from an affected user initially failed immediately with “Access denied” and did not appear in the queue until an explicit Deny ACE was removed, and the physical device was reported as “not responding” (print jobs for other users either remained in the queue or disappeared without printing). These failures occurred after a recent network change that moved the device into a separate printer VLAN, producing both connectivity/availability issues and authentication/permission problems. 

Taken together, the evidence indicates a combined problem: an explicit Deny on the printer/queue prevented job acceptance at the print-server/ACL layer, while the VLAN move introduced network reachability or firewall/port-blocking issues that caused the device to appear offline for some print paths. The immediate remediation actions noted were removal of the Deny and correction of the group/GPO configuration, after which the printer became the correct default for the impacted users.

Recommended follow-ups: validate printer ACLs and AD/group membership for deployed print GPOs; confirm routing/firewall rules and necessary ports between the print server, clients and the new printer VLAN; verify printer port configuration (SMB/LPR/IPPS) and DNS/IPP resolution; run end-to-end print tests from representative clients; and document/coordinate VLAN or network segmentation changes with print and identity owners to prevent recurrence.

title: PRINT & SCANNING INFRASTRUCTURE STANDARD – VERSION 1.0
description: The "PRINT & SCANNING INFRASTRUCTURE STANDARD – VERSION 1.0" is the documented, organizational standard and authoritative blueprint referenced during troubleshooting, ticketing, analysis and remediation. It prescribes scan‑to‑mail architecture and operational controls: printers must not send directly to the Internet and must use a dedicated SMTP relay that permits only defined IP ranges; DNS resolution for relays must be performed internally; authentication must not be bypassed; maximum scanned file sizes must align with mail infrastructure limits; and any mail‑server policy changes must be coordinated with scanning systems. It also mandates printer provisioning and permission practices: printer access is assigned exclusively via Active Directory groups (no individual user permissions at the queue level), NTFS/share permissions must be consistent, printers are assigned by location using AD groups, default printers per location are set via Group Policy, and parallel groups with identical function are prohibited. The standard was used as the authoritative guide for remediation; permissions were cleaned up and scan profiles/mail‑limit settings were adjusted in accordance with its requirements.

title: FINANCE DEPARTMENT
description: Organizational department affected by the issue; several employees within this department experienced incorrect default printer assignment after an AD group reassignment during a reorganization.

title: NEW FINANCE AD GROUP
description: The new Active Directory group into which the Finance team was moved during a reorganization. The old group was deactivated. The new group inadvertently had membership in a higher-level Zurich location AD group, causing unintended GPO-based printer mappings.

title: ZURICH LOCATION AD GROUP
description: A higher-level Active Directory/location group representing Zurich. Membership of the Finance AD group in this Zurich group caused the Zurich printer mapping to apply to Finance users via Group Policy.

title: GROUP POLICY (GPO)
description: GROUP POLICY (GPO): Active Directory Group Policy objects are used to provision and automatically assign printers and print queues to user devices by location or department, set default printers per location, enforce Point-and-Print restrictions, and apply other printer-related configuration settings. In this environment printers were mapped based on AD group membership; overlapping AD group memberships caused the Zurich printer to be applied as the default for Basel users. GPO application and update are therefore key troubleshooting steps in the decision tree—remediation in the reported incident consisted of correcting AD group membership and forcing a policy refresh so the intended location-based printer assignments and Point-and-Print restrictions were correctly applied.

title: GPUPDATE /FORCE
description: Administrative command executed on the affected workstation(s) to force immediate Group Policy refresh. Used during testing after temporary removal of incorrect group membership; after gpupdate /force and user re-login the default printer remained correctly set to MFP-BASEL-01.

title: OLD FINANCE AD GROUP
description: The prior Active Directory group for the Finance department that was deactivated during the reorganization. Its deactivation and replacement by the NEW FINANCE AD GROUP are part of the change that led to the inconsistent group membership and printer mapping.

title: BASEL LOCATION
description: BASEL LOCATION is the customer's physical Basel office and the site where the reported issue occurred. The Basel site had a recently implemented Voice VLAN and network behavior that differed from other sites (for example Zurich) in NAT/UDP session timeout settings. BASEL LOCATION was also the intended scope for the multifunction printer MFP-BASEL-01 and was the actual physical location of the affected users whose default printer should be the Basel-based device.

title: ZURICH LOCATION
description: ZURICH LOCATION (Customer Company 52 AG) is the physical Zurich office site and the scope of the incident in which roughly 12 users experienced a media restriction / call issue. A targeted firewall change (security hardening) was applied at this site and, because Media Bypass behavior was in effect, that change directly impacted media flows and was later identified as the root cause of the media restriction. The site is also represented in Active Directory by the ZURICH LOCATION AD GROUP and associated GPOs that map the Zurich default printer (MFP-ZUERICH-01) to members of the location; that AD configuration unintentionally included the Finance group via nested group membership. A known-good/reference configuration for Zurich showed a 180-second UDP/session timeout and did not experience call drops, indicating the affected Zurich firewall or timeout settings were inconsistent with the baseline configuration.

title: AFFECTED USERS
description: Set of multiple Finance-department employees at the Basel location who experienced the default printer automatically switching to the Zurich device. These users were tested during remediation and validated that the fix resolved the issue.

title: AFFECTED WORKSTATION
description: Representative workstation used by an affected user that was remotely connected for troubleshooting. On this workstation the default printer was incorrectly set to MFP-ZUERICH-01, Device Manager showed both printers, and gpupdate /force was executed during testing.

title: PRINTER PROVISIONING
description: PRINTER PROVISIONING: PRINTER PROVISIONING is the operational procedure and provisioning policy for bringing printers online in the environment. It requires creating a print queue on the Windows print server, configuring and deploying a standardized/approved driver, and assigning printers by Active Directory group membership. Assignments and default-printer behavior are enforced per location via Group Policy Objects (GPOs) that target AD groups. The process is governed by the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and configurations are validated after updates to ensure consistent, centrally managed printer behavior.

title: TI-1623488
description: Support incident opened 02.12.25 for a single user in the Basel office who could not print while other users in the same room could print. Ticket created via phone, assigned to Employee 27, closed 03.12.25. Closure note: "Permission on printer queue corrected according to Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard. Printing possible again." Activities include remote session, permission review, removal of an explicit Deny on the printer queue, test prints and closure by Consulting Sample Company.

title: CONTACT PERSON 20
description: Reporter of the incident (Mr. Contact Person 20) who called in the issue describing that one employee could not print while others in the same office could; acted as customer liaison for the ticket.

title: EMPLOYEE 27
description: EMPLOYEE 27 is a Consulting Sample Company support engineer who served as the ticket owner/assigned engineer for incident TI-1623488. They were assigned the ticket on 29.11.24 and set its status to "In Progress" on 02.12.24. Across the incident lifecycle they performed initial troubleshooting (permission checks, browser/cache tests, group membership checks), escalated for deeper analysis when behavior was inconsistent, and coordinated with the Network Team and Core Team. They contacted Contact Person 16 and conducted hands-on troubleshooting on the customer workstation (including login tests for FTP/SFTP using WinSCP), documented findings, and performed a remote connection to investigate printer queue permissions. EMPLOYEE 27 removed an explicit Deny entry affecting the user, validated printing with a test print, and closed the ticket; the ticket was also, at one point during the workflow, transferred to Employee 26.

title: AFFECTED USER
description: Unnamed employee in the Basel office who experienced immediate "Access denied" when attempting to print; printing worked for colleagues in the same room. User account membership in AD group "PRT-Basel" was confirmed, but an explicit Deny entry on the printer queue prevented printing.

title: PRINT SERVER
type: SERVER INFRASTRUCTURE (APPLICATION SERVERS, DOMAIN CONTROLLERS, VIRTUAL MACHINES)
description: PRINT SERVER is the logical/server role that hosts printer queues and standardized drivers, runs the Print Spooler service, and acts as the central point for printer provisioning, queue management and accepting print jobs from user devices. It hosts the queue for MFP-BASEL-01 and is responsible for enforcing printer queue permissions (an administrator review found an Active Directory group and explicit Deny entries on that queue). Operationally the print server receives and forwards jobs to physical printers; troubleshooting showed jobs briefly appearing then disappearing and that direct test prints from the server to the printer initially failed until a network rule was adjusted, indicating both queue permissions (AD groups/explicit Deny) and network/firewall rules can affect print delivery.

title: PRINTER QUEUE (MFP-BASEL-01 QUEUE)
description: Logical print queue on the print server for MFP-BASEL-01. Reviewed during troubleshooting; configured to grant print permission to AD groups (e.g., PRT-Basel) and contained an additional explicit Deny ACE for the affected user originating from a prior test configuration.

title: PRT-BASEL (AD GROUP)
description: Active Directory security group used to assign print permissions for users in the Basel location. Confirmed membership for the affected user and granted print permission on the printer queue per the environment's permission model.

title: EXPLICIT DENY PERMISSION (PRINTER QUEUE ACE)
description: An explicit Access Control Entry (ACE) configured on the printer queue denying the affected user the Print right. This deny entry overrode group-based allow permissions and was the root cause of the user's "Access denied" error; it was removed during remediation.

title: NTFS AND SHARE PERMISSIONS
description: Filesystem and network share permission requirements referenced by the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard; the standard mandates consistency between NTFS and share permissions and that access to print services be controlled via AD group membership rather than individual ACEs on the queue.

title: USER WORKSTATION
description: USER WORKSTATION: The affected new employee's Windows 11 desktop/laptop endpoint where Group Policy processing occurred. An administrator ran gpupdate /force and the user logged in; the workstation received a Group Policy–delivered printer mapping for the network queue MFP-BASEL-01. A remote support connection was established to the endpoint to troubleshoot printing. Initial attempts to print to MFP-BASEL-01 returned immediate "Access denied" responses due to incorrect permissions on the print queue/server; once those permissions were corrected during the support session, the workstation successfully performed a test print.

title: COLLEAGUE WORKSTATION
description: A colleague's desktop/laptop in the same Basel office used as a comparison/test device; printing from this workstation succeeded, demonstrating the issue was specific to the affected user's account/permissions rather than the physical printer or network connectivity.

title: PRINTER ACL
description: Access Control List (ACL) applied to the MFP-BASEL-01 printer queue on the print server. The ACL contains Access Control Entries (ACEs) — including group Allow entries (e.g., PRT-Basel) and an explicit Deny ACE for the affected user originating from a prior test — which determine whether print jobs are accepted or rejected.

title: QUEUE-LEVEL PERMISSIONS
description: The permission model applied at the printer queue level (on the print server) that enforces who can submit print jobs. Per the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard, these should be controlled via AD groups (no individual user ACEs); in this incident a misconfigured queue-level ACE (Explicit Deny) blocked the affected user.

title: TI-858473
description: Support incident opened 26.06.24 (Title: KF12: Hacker Attack Store). Created via Consulting Sample Company portal (origin: Internet). Company: Customer Company 12. Contact: Contact Person 10. Owner/assignee: Employee 15. Priority: HIGH. Status: set In Progress on 05.07.24 and closed 22.07.24 with closure text "Incident has been closed." Ticket contains portal notes, PDF attachments with malicious links, and investigative notes describing credential-harvesting phishing and requested mitigations.

title: CUSTOMER COMPANY 12
description: Customer tenant/organization affected by the incident "Hacker Attack Store". Domain referenced implicitly in service-account addresses and requested exclusion list; the organization reported automatic downloads of two PDF files containing links to a fake Microsoft login page and requested mitigations/be assessed for damage.

title: CONTACT PERSON 10
description: Named customer contact who received initial acknowledgements and communications regarding the ticket; target of initial confirmation emails and communications from Consulting Sample Company staff.

title: EMPLOYEE 15
description: EMPLOYEE 15 is a support engineer and assigned ticket owner who authored investigative portal notes and managed remediation and closure activities across multiple incidents. For TI-858473 they documented the incident impact (noting that the "incident... cannot assess the damage due to missing software & services"), proposed a mitigation — a firewall/geolocation rule targeting non‑MFA users — and added exclusions and follow-up details to the ticket. For TI-1621089 they performed hands‑on troubleshooting of a user/printer issue: reviewed the user account and Active Directory group membership, verified Group Policy (GPO) status, executed gpupdate /force, had the user re‑logon, validated the printer mapping and performed a test print, then closed the ticket and communicated the resolution to the customer (Consulting Sample Company).

title: EMPLOYEE 16
description: EMPLOYEE 16 is a Consulting Sample Company helpdesk/triage/support agent who acted as the initial contact and ticket creator for the incident. They opened and acknowledged the ticket (recorded as "Ticket opened by Employee 16" on 26.11.24), created the CRM web/admin entry on 29.11.24 at 07:49, and performed the initial ticket actions and notes. EMPLOYEE 16 communicated receipt to stakeholders (including Contact Person 10 and Contact Person 16) in the initial email activity, asked to check who set up the FileZilla Server, and forwarded the matter to the responsible system engineer for resolution.

title: HELPDESK
description:
HELPDESK — the frontline support team at Consulting Sample Company — is the initial point of contact for incidents: they receive inbound emails, create and issue ticket notifications (appearing in the To/cc of original messages and in portal metadata/workflow), and perform first‑line ticket routing, triage and communications with customers and recorded ticket activities. HELPDESK conducts standard initial diagnostic questioning (scope, duration, affected locations/users, example numbers/times, recent changes or Windows updates, OS and browser type, device scope, whether headsets or integrated mics are used) and basic client tests (Teams test calls, internet/packet‑loss checks, LAN/WLAN validation). They perform routine troubleshooting steps (cache clear, client reinstall, driver checks), coordinate or run remote checks with users, and document all actions in the ticket. 

When issues indicate deeper causes, HELPDESK escalates to the appropriate teams — engineering, BU Unified Communications/Direct Routing analysis, or carrier coordination — and remains a contact point during deployments (for example, Intune) and post‑remediation verification. They handle customer‑facing follow‑up (closure communications, feedback/survey requests), and often close tickets after remediation and verification, ensuring the incident record reflects the triage, tests and escalation history.

title: APPLICATIONUSER (CRM DEVELOPMENT)
description: System/application account or automated portal actor (noted as "ApplicationUser, CRM Development") that authored or logged a portal entry suggesting a "Lessons Learned" meeting and proposed security topics after the incident.

title: FIREWALL RULE (ROUTE NON‑MFA USERS VIA GEO CHECK)
type: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE (FIREWALLS, VLANS, NAT, LOAD BALANCERS)
description: Requested network-level mitigation described by Employee 15: define a firewall rule that routes users who are not using MFA through a geolocation control first. Intended to block or add controls for suspicious/non‑MFA sign-ins from specified locations, with exceptions for listed accounts.

title: GEOLOCATION CHECK FOR NON‑MFA LOGINS
description: Access-policy style control requested to apply geolocation checks to sign-in attempts of users who are not using MFA. Scope described: apply geolocation checks for Switzerland, Germany, Austria, Italy; intended to add an additional decision point for non‑MFA sign‑ins.

title: IP BLOCK 20.26.116.69
type: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE (FIREWALLS, VLANS, NAT, LOAD BALANCERS)
description: Specific IPv4 address the customer asked to be blocked as part of immediate containment/mitigation; provided as a candidate block in the firewall/geolocation control request.

title: S_KEEPIT@CUSTOMERCOMPANY12.CH
description: Customer-supplied exception account to be excluded from the proposed firewall/geolocation restriction; listed in Employee 15's mitigation request.

title: SU-POS-APP-KF12CARD@CUSTOMERCOMPANY12.CH
description: Customer-supplied exception/service account intended to be excluded from the restriction; likely an application/service integration account used by the customer.

title: SU-MDEAPP@CUSTOMERCOMPANY12.CH
description: Another service account listed as an exception to the proposed restriction; likely used by customer services or integrations.

title: D365INTPROD@CUSTOMERCOMPANY12.CH
description: Additional customer integration/service account later requested by Employee 15 to be excluded from the restriction; name indicates a Dynamics 365 integration/service identity.

title: SIGN‑IN ACTIVITIES
description: Authentication/sign-in telemetry concept referenced in the mitigation request ("Only block 'Sign‑in Activities' performed by individuals – not those executed by Microsoft services"). Refers to authentication logs/events (Azure AD/Audit logs) the customer wants to target, distinguishing human sign-ins from system/service sign-ins.

title: MFA
description: MFA (Multi‑factor authentication) is the authentication control used in the Train integration, and its behavior was central to the incident/failure analysis. MFA requests for the Toolbox component are orchestrated by ADFS client settings: when the RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders option is enabled, MFA can be delegated to external claims providers which return “external MFA claims” that satisfy the requirement. In practice the system treats MFA status as a protective criterion and a control factor that determines login exceptions or routing: users who do not use MFA are planned to be handled differently (for example, non‑MFA sign‑ins routed through geolocation checks as a mitigation). Ticket remediation focused on adjusting ADFS client parameters to change MFA behavior and the delegation to external claims providers so that the desired MFA enforcement and exception handling (including geolocation checks for non‑MFA users) are applied.

title: ALGINA.CO
description: Malicious domain identified as the credential exfiltration endpoint: user credentials submitted to the fake login pages were transmitted to algina[.]co according to the investigative notes. Domain was used by the phishing/credential-harvesting flow before redirecting victims to Microsoft to appear legitimate."

title: FAKE MICROSOFT LOGIN PAGE
description: Phishing webpage (linked from two attached PDF files) that mimics a Microsoft sign-in page; after credential submission it relays credentials to ALGINA.CO and then redirects to Microsoft to reduce suspicion. Purpose: credential harvesting to enable further attacks against Customer Company 12."

title: PDF ATTACHMENTS (DOWNLO_732783232, DOWNLO_732783232 (1))
description: Two PDF files uploaded/downloaded via the CRM portal that contained links to the fake Microsoft login page; portal notes indicate these were automatically downloaded and later blocked/disabled. The PDFs are the initial delivery vector for the phishing links.

title: LESSONS LEARNED / PROPOSED TOPICS
description: Post-incident action item logged by ApplicationUser/CRM Development recommending a Lessons Learned meeting to define sustainable security measures; proposed topics include MusterFirmaguard, ModernSecOps, and SOC (security operations center) setup — these are recommended organizational/process changes to improve future incident response and detection.

title: MUSTERFIRMAGUARD
description: Proposed security measure/tool name listed as a topic for the Lessons Learned meeting—likely a solution or product to be evaluated as part of improving detection/prevention.

title: MODERNSECOPS
description: Proposed operational approach/topic for the Lessons Learned meeting: modern security operations practices (detection, response, automation) to improve incident handling.

title: SOC
description: Recommendation to consider a Security Operations Center (SOC) as a sustainable security measure and operational capability following the credential-harvesting incident.

title: ADMIN (CRM)
description: CRM administrative actor referenced in portal notes ("Admin, CRM") who performed ticket assignment actions and portal-based operations (assigned ticket to BU Customer Services Run and to Employee 15). Represents the CRM admin role/account that changed assignments and recorded metadata.

title: CRM (APPLICATION)
description: CRM system/application used as the customer-facing portal (referenced generically as "CRM" in portal notes). Closely related to Microsoft CRM Portals and used to create the ticket and store uploaded attachments.

title: MITARBEITER15@CONSULTINGSAMPLECOMPANY.CH
description: Email/service account address that received portal notifications and NO-REPLY notes (appears in portal activity lines). Associated with Employee 15 / Consulting Sample Company and used as a notification recipient for the ticket and portal events.

title: TI-1602241
description: TI-1602241 is a support incident for Customer Company 52 AG (ticket KF52: "Microsoft Teams – External calls without audio (only silence)"), opened 08.07.25 via phone and initially assigned to Employee 33. The report described one‑way audio on external Teams calls: external participants could hear internal users, but internal users heard no audio. The case was progressed to In Progress and escalated to BU Unified Communications, where System Engineer 18 took over investigation. Troubleshooting documented firewall log review, SBC log analysis, temporary test calls and monitoring; the investigation identified a media‑flow restriction caused by a recent firewall change at the Zurich location. Configuration was corrected to conform with the Direct Routing architecture, voice transmission was validated as stable, and the ticket was resolved and closed on 18.07.25 with the closure note: "Analysis completed. Configuration adjusted according to Direct Routing architecture. Voice transmission stable again." Contact for the record is Contact Person 14.

title: CUSTOMER COMPANY 52 AG
description: CUSTOMER COMPANY 52 AG is the customer/tenant (referred to as KF52 in an authored blueprint) affected by a series of Microsoft Teams and telephony incidents and for whom multiple support tickets were opened (including TI-1615096 and TI-1616128). Incidents included one-way audio on external PSTN calls via Microsoft Teams Direct Routing at the Zurich site (about a dozen users could not hear callers), missed incoming calls across the company from a newly migrated carrier number range (the carrier migration was confirmed and resolution validated after remediation), and an issue where external project partners appeared permanently "Offline" in Teams, degrading partner communications (reported by Contact Person 18). Affected employees and named contacts (including Contact Person 11 and Contact Person 07) belong to CUSTOMER COMPANY 52 AG; impacted locations include Zurich and Basel and an approximately six‑user Sales group. Remediation and ticket handling were performed on the company’s behalf, with Consulting Sample Company and an external consulting support organization involved for specific sites/teams. Separately, a tenant blueprint (KF52) prescribes how CUSTOMER COMPANY 52 AG’s Teams tenant should be configured for federation, external access and presence sharing while meeting the company’s security and data‑protection requirements.

title: CONTACT PERSON 14
description: CONTACT PERSON 14 served as the company’s named customer contact and primary liaison for the incident. They reported the issue by phone, provided follow‑up information, received ongoing updates, and supplied details about the affected scope and call behavior: the problem involved external calls only (both incoming and outgoing) and impacted roughly a dozen users at the Zurich location.

title: EMPLOYEE 33
description: EMPLOYEE 33 is a support engineer and ticket owner who handled multiple user-facing incidents and coordinated escalations and hand‑overs. For ticket TI-1602241 they performed initial troubleshooting (including client restart and cache clear), asked diagnostic questions, coordinated further tests, and managed escalation and resolution activities. As owner of TI-1616128 they connected via remote session, inspected Microsoft Teams and Windows audio settings, identified and remediated a physical headset issue by reconnecting the microphone cable, verified success with a test call, and closed the ticket. As owner of TI-1615042 they performed a remote session, applied permission changes in accordance with the team Blueprint, verified camera availability, and closed the ticket. Throughout these engagements EMPLOYEE 33 combined remote troubleshooting, hardware validation, configuration/permission changes, and escalation coordination to resolve issues.

title: SYSTEM ENGINEER 18
description: SYSTEM ENGINEER 18 took over the ticket on 09.07.25 and performed in‑depth Direct Routing troubleshooting and media‑flow analysis. The engineer reviewed SBC (Session Border Controller) logs and the Microsoft Teams Admin Center, communicated findings to the customer, coordinated required configuration changes with networking and unified‑communications teams, and implemented configuration adjustments in accordance with the Direct Routing architecture.

title: S. MEIER
description: End user used for test calls; internal Teams calls worked for this user, external calls established but the user reported hearing nothing while the external party could hear him (used to reproduce and observe one-directional audio behaviour).

title: UNIFIED COMMUNICATIONS / NETWORK TEAM
description: Team that the issue was escalated to for analysis/correction of Direct Routing and media flow. Responsible for SBC/media and firewall configuration coordination.

title: SBC
type: UNIFIED COMMUNICATIONS / DIRECT ROUTING (SBC, MEDIA FLOW)
description: SBC (Session Border Controller): The SBC instance(s) in the Direct Routing path (monitored as Online in the Microsoft Teams Admin Center) show successful SIP signaling and session establishment—SIP INVITE/200 OK exchanges and no call drops per SBC logs and SIP/SDP analysis—yet media is impaired: Microsoft-originating RTP is visible to the SBC while client-originating RTP is not consistently visible, producing one-way audio (media flowing only in one direction). This points to a media-path issue (not signaling), commonly caused by client-side NAT/firewall/RTP port blocking, asymmetric routing or RTP pinholing problems, or media-anchoring/misconfiguration on the SBC or edge network.

title: BLUEPRINT – MICROSOFT TEAMS DIRECT ROUTING & MEDIA FLOW – VERSION 2.1
description: BLUEPRINT – MICROSOFT TEAMS DIRECT ROUTING & MEDIA FLOW – VERSION 2.1 is an internal architecture and configuration blueprint used as the authoritative specification during analysis and remediation of Teams Direct Routing deployments. It documents Media Bypass behavior, RTP media port range requirements, NAT traversal considerations, location-specific firewall rules, and end-to-end media flow expectations, and includes Direct Routing configuration guidance. Operations and network engineering teams use this blueprint to validate and correct firewall configurations, NAT rules, and overall media routing to ensure compliant and reliable Teams media connectivity.

title: ZURICH FIREWALL RULE
type: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE (FIREWALLS, VLANS, NAT, LOAD BALANCERS)
description: Specific firewall rule implemented at the Zurich location as part of a security hardening change over the weekend. The new outbound rule was more restrictive and did not permit the full media port range required for Teams Direct Routing, causing partial/media-blocking behaviour.

title: MEDIA PORT RANGES
type: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE (FIREWALLS, VLANS, NAT, LOAD BALANCERS)
description: Range of UDP ports used by media (RTP) for Microsoft Teams Direct Routing. The implemented firewall rule deviated from Microsoft/internal blueprint port ranges, which led to blocked media traffic.

title: MEDIA FLOW
type: UNIFIED COMMUNICATIONS / DIRECT ROUTING (SBC, MEDIA FLOW)
description: The RTP-based audio media path for external calls. Investigation showed media negotiation (SDP) occurred but RTP/actual audio was only stable in one direction because of firewall restrictions. Restoring full media port allowances restored bidirectional media flow.

title: RTP TRAFFIC
type: UNIFIED COMMUNICATIONS / DIRECT ROUTING (SBC, MEDIA FLOW)
description: Real-time Transport Protocol streams carrying audio payload. Observed to be stable only in one direction during the incident; directly impacted by blocked media ports on the firewall.

title: SIP
type: UNIFIED COMMUNICATIONS / DIRECT ROUTING (SBC, MEDIA FLOW)
description: Session Initiation Protocol signaling used to establish calls. SIP signaling was observed as successful (calls established) and not blocked by the firewall rule, distinguishing the issue as media-related rather than signaling.

title: SDP
type: UNIFIED COMMUNICATIONS / DIRECT ROUTING (SBC, MEDIA FLOW)
description: Session Description Protocol used during call setup for media negotiation. SDP negotiation occurred, but resultant RTP streams were partially blocked due to firewall port restrictions.

title: MICROSOFT TEAMS
description: MICROSOFT TEAMS — The customer’s enterprise collaboration and unified‑communications platform (desktop and web clients) was at the center of the incident. Internal Teams‑to‑Teams chat, video and calls continued to work, but external PSTN traffic arriving via Direct Routing was disrupted. Symptoms included one‑way audio (in some cases affected users could not hear external PSTN callers; in other cases external participants could not hear the user), PSTN calls disconnecting after roughly 30 seconds, and Teams showing the client as “Connected” even when audio was missing. Device abnormalities were observed on affected clients: Teams listed “USB Audio Device” as the selected microphone yet no input was recorded (a test call initially failed and later succeeded after hardware reconnection), and the camera did not appear in device settings until OS/app camera permissions were adjusted. Presence sharing for external partners was displayed incorrectly as “Offline,” and chat delivery and call signaling to/from external parties were impacted; the Teams client did continue to receive messages but in‑app banner notifications had been disabled (later re‑enabled during remediation). Troubleshooting used Teams Admin Center and Teams PowerShell, reviewed Direct Routing/SBC signaling logs, and validated Teams license/voice configuration (Calling Plan vs Direct Routing); the Teams Admin Center showed no incoming calls for some affected numbers, confirming Teams as the ultimate inbound routing target. Microsoft Teams was also used as an operational communications channel during the incident response (including on 30.11.24), and configuration adjustments consistent with an internal Federation blueprint were applied as part of the investigation and remediation.

title: HEADSETS (JABRA, POLY)
description: Hardware used by affected users (different vendors noted: Jabra, Poly). Variation of headsets across affected users indicated the problem was not caused by a single headset model.

title: LAN CONNECTION
type: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE (FIREWALLS, VLANS, NAT, LOAD BALANCERS)
description: LAN CONNECTION — At the Zurich site the affected users were connected via LAN CONNECTION (not home office or WLAN). Multiple tests using LAN clients and different headsets reproduced the problem, which persisted across wired endpoints. This behavior localizes the fault to the Zurich LAN edge and adjacent infrastructure (for example local switches, routers/firewall, or edge network services) rather than to wireless, remote/home networks, or specific client/headset hardware.

title: FIREWALL CHANGE (SECURITY HARDENING)
type: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE (FIREWALLS, VLANS, NAT, LOAD BALANCERS)
description: Weekend security hardening change implemented at the Zurich site that introduced a new, more restrictive outbound firewall rule; this change is the operational event that triggered the media-blocking behaviour and investigation.

title: OUTBOUND TRAFFIC RULE
type: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE (FIREWALLS, VLANS, NAT, LOAD BALANCERS)
description: The specific outbound firewall rule applied at the Zurich location that restricted outbound media ports more tightly than required by Direct Routing/Teams, causing RTP/media to be partially blocked.

title: TEMPORARY FIREWALL ADJUSTMENT
type: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE (FIREWALLS, VLANS, NAT, LOAD BALANCERS)
description: A temporary relaxation/adjustment of the Zurich firewall to open media ports for testing. This change restored bidirectional audio during tests but was later reverted back to the restrictive rule, causing the issue to reappear until a permanent correction was applied.

title: MEDIA BYPASS (LOCATION SETTING)
type: UNIFIED COMMUNICATIONS / DIRECT ROUTING (SBC, MEDIA FLOW)
description: Direct Routing/location configuration option (Media Bypass) present at the Zurich site; referenced in the blueprint review as relevant to how media is routed and which media port ranges and firewall rules must be allowed for correct RTP flow.

title: LOCATION CONFIGURATION (MEDIA BYPASS ENABLED)
description: The site-specific configuration context for the Zurich location (noted in the blueprint review) showing Media Bypass enabled; used to determine the correct firewall/media-port requirements for that site.

title: TI-1624175
description: Support incident "KF60: Scan-to-Mail works only for internal recipients" opened 10.12.25 for Customer Company 60 AG (created via Email). Reporter: Contact Person 25. Owner/assignee: Employee 30. Issue: scans from a multifunction device at the Zurich location were delivered to internal mailboxes but not to external recipients. Investigations (mail log analysis, VLAN and DNS checks) performed; closure on 18.12.25: "SMTP relay and DNS configuration adjusted according to Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard. Scan-to-Mail fully restored." Ticket records coordination with Network and Mail teams and tests to external recipients (Gmail, partner).

title: CONTACT PERSON 25
description: Named contact at Customer Company 60 AG who reported the Scan-to-Mail issue from the Zurich location, supplied initial symptoms and responded to inquiries during troubleshooting.

title: EMPLOYEE 30
description: Support engineer/owner assigned to TI-1624175 responsible for coordinating troubleshooting, escalation/coordination with Network and Mail teams, and closure of the ticket after configuration changes and testing.

title: MULTIFUNCTION DEVICE
description: The printer/scanner device at the Zurich location used for Scan-to-Mail. Behavior: scans deliver successfully to internal recipients but external deliveries were failing. Device showed no local error messages. It has an IP address assigned in a newly defined printer VLAN; testing after remediation proved successful to external recipients.

title: SMTP RELAY
description: SMTP RELAY is a dedicated SMTP relay used to deliver outbound mail from printers, multifunction devices and internal scanning services (Scan-to-Mail) to internal and external recipients. It is configured to accept connections only from defined IP ranges (e.g., a printer VLAN) and to forward mail onward; misconfiguration produced “Relay denied” log entries when the printer IP range was not allowed, and adding the printer VLAN to the allowed range restored external deliveries. The relay requires authentication with a service account and is secured with TLS. It enforces a maximum message-size limit (recently reduced to 10 MB), which generated “Message size exceeds fixed limit” log entries and caused larger scanned PDFs to be rejected before they reached the internal mail server. Reachability checks and inspection of the mail queue are standard parts of Scan-to-Mail troubleshooting for this SMTP RELAY.

title: PRINTER VLAN
type: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE (FIREWALLS, VLANS, NAT, LOAD BALANCERS)
description: PRINTER VLAN — A dedicated, VLAN-separated network segment (recently created at the Basel location) where printers’ IP addresses now reside. Because printers are isolated by VLAN, clients on other networks cannot reach them unless inter-VLAN routing and firewall/ACLs permit it; network reachability and simple pingability to the printer IPs are used as primary troubleshooting checks for print connectivity. When the new PRINTER VLAN was deployed, the print server (located on a different server network) initially could not reach the printers until appropriate routing and firewall/ACL rules were created. Separately, the PRINTER VLAN’s IP range had not been added to the organization’s SMTP relay allow list, which caused external SMTP relay attempts from devices in that VLAN to be denied until the VLAN range was whitelisted.

title: DNS SERVICE / DNS FORWARDING
description: Local DNS configuration at the location. Customer reported that new DNS forwarding was configured recently. The Print & Scanning blueprint requires internal DNS resolution for Scan-to-Mail flows; DNS forwarding changes at the location were investigated as part of the incident to ensure DNS resolution behavior met blueprint requirements.

title: EXTERNAL MAIL SECURITY SOLUTION
description: An external mail security/filtering solution at the customer environment that was adjusted the week prior to the incident. Its recent changes were noted as a possible factor during initial inquiry and investigation of mail delivery paths and DNS/relay behavior.

title: SMTP LOGS
description: Mail/SMTP logs analyzed during troubleshooting. Logs showed the error "Relay denied" for external delivery attempts originating from the printer's IP, indicating the SMTP relay refused to forward messages from that source/IP range.

title: NETWORK TEAM
description: NETWORK TEAM is the internal network engineering group responsible for network configuration, firewall rules, VLAN and NAT policies, and network-level diagnostics. They were engaged by customers and internal stakeholders (addressed as “Hello Network Team” in initial customer mail) to review firewall settings and confirm that the new printer VLAN had been omitted from the SMTP relay allow list; they coordinated with the Mail Team to update relay/SMTP permissions. The team coordinated and implemented VLAN/NAT changes at the Basel site, applied an adjusted NAT/UDP session timeout to align with the Direct Routing architecture, and performed firewall analysis and troubleshooting for FTP access. They worked directly with Employee 21 to confirm and correct an omitted firewall rule to restore connectivity between the print server and the printer VLAN, and collaborated with Employee 11 to adjust firewall rules to restore communication for the NDES connector.

title: MAIL TEAM
description: MAIL TEAM is the customer/operations group responsible for the organization’s mail server and SMTP relay policy and infrastructure. Two weeks prior they reduced the maximum email size to help limit spam; during a later remediation they worked with an external consulting team to moderately raise the SMTP relay size limit. They also coordinated with the Network Team and support staff to update the SMTP relay configuration and to release the printer VLAN IP range so printers could send outbound mail to external recipients. Overall, MAIL TEAM manages SMTP relay controls and cross‑functional changes to balance spam mitigation and legitimate outbound mail delivery.

title: GMAIL
description: External recipient mail provider used as a test target during validation; test scans to Gmail succeeded after remediation, demonstrating restored external delivery.

title: PARTNER COMPANY
description: External corporate recipient used as a test target; successful delivery to the partner company after SMTP relay configuration changes demonstrated resolution for non-consumer external recipients as well.

title: INTERNAL ROUTING
type: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE (FIREWALLS, VLANS, NAT, LOAD BALANCERS)
description: Internal network routing at the Zurich location that carries traffic between local VLANs and internal services. The record states "Internal routing works," indicating internal routing allowed the multifunction device to reach internal mail infrastructure while external relay forwarding was blocked.

title: PRINT SERVICES FOR SAP OUTPUT STANDARD VERSION 1.0
description: Approved standard (Version 1.0, Date: 25.07.2024) that defines the provisioning and operational guidelines for SAP output printing at Customer Company 13. Covers architecture, roles, operational principles, decision tree and post-change validation steps (queue status, spooler, sharing/publishing, test printing). Focuses on consistency of queue properties, network sharing/publishing mechanisms, and VM/update-related restore procedures. Cites a real-world pattern observed in ticket TI-931293 and mandates priority handling for SAP print disruptions.

title: CUSTOMER COMPANY 13
description: CUSTOMER COMPANY 13 (also referenced as Customer Company 13 AG) is the customer organization that reported the incident and that owns and operates the SAP landscape, SAP printing, and the onsite print infrastructure, including the central Windows print servers described in the standard. Multiple named contacts and stakeholders — Contact Person 11, Contact Person 12, and Tom — are employees of CUSTOMER COMPANY 13. The document’s scope covers the provisioning and operation of SAP outputs for CUSTOMER COMPANY 13.

title: TI-931293
description: TI-931293 is a high-priority support ticket (Ticket name: "KF13: PRIO HIGH - Printing from SAP not possible") opened 18.07.24 for Customer Company 13 after a phone report from Contact Person 11. The ticket was created in the CRM by Employee 23, assigned to Owner Employee 17, moved to "In Progress" and was resolved/closed on 19.07.24. The incident prevented order printing from SAP and thereby blocked deliveries; it exhibited classic post–Windows update/VM-restore printing symptoms (stuck SAP jobs, disappearing jobs from the queue, or jobs accumulating on the print server requiring manual intervention). The closure note states that printer network-share issues caused by a Windows update and subsequent VM restore were corrected and printing was restored. This ticket is also cited in an internal document as an example case of such post-update printing failures and to justify classifying similar incidents as PRIO HIGH.

title: SAP SYSTEM
description: The enterprise SAP landscape responsible for generating business print jobs (delivery notes, pick lists, labels). Within the architecture it hands jobs to SAP Spool / Output Management and defines SAP print targets (logical printers) and print paths that are business-critical.

title: SAP SPOOL / OUTPUT MANAGEMENT
description: SAP subsystem that generates and manages print jobs and transfers them to defined SAP print targets; a primary source of print jobs and a chokepoint if print targets or paths are misconfigured or unavailable.

title: SAP PRINT TARGET
description: Logical printer definitions within SAP (print targets) that map SAP output to Windows print servers/queues; critical configuration element in the SAP → Windows printing flow.

title: WINDOWS PRINTSERVER (SAP PRINTSERVER)
type: SERVER INFRASTRUCTURE (APPLICATION SERVERS, DOMAIN CONTROLLERS, VIRTUAL MACHINES)
description: Central Windows servers dedicated to processing SAP print jobs (called SAP Printservers in the document). Example hostnames used in the ticket context: VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04. Hosts print queues, runs the Print Spooler service, and exposes shared/published queues to SAP."

title: VRINFRGERPRNT03
type: SERVER INFRASTRUCTURE (APPLICATION SERVERS, DOMAIN CONTROLLERS, VIRTUAL MACHINES)
description: VRINFRGERPRNT03 is a dedicated Windows print‑server virtual machine identified in the document as one of the SAP print servers hosting SAP-related print queues and is subject to the organisation's standard post‑update/restore validation. During the reported incident, SAP printing via VRINFRGERPRNT03 failed; technicians attempted to restart the server and the Windows Print Spooler without resolving the issue. A restore to a pre‑maintenance state was then performed on the VM, and during subsequent print testing the server experienced blue screens (system crashes). The failure to recover after service and server restarts, followed by crashes after a restore, indicates the problem persisted beyond simple spooler faults and may point to OS‑level instability, driver or spooler corruption, or VM/host‑level issues that require deeper diagnostics and controlled post‑restore validation per the standard.

title: VRINFRGERPRNT04
type: SERVER INFRASTRUCTURE (APPLICATION SERVERS, DOMAIN CONTROLLERS, VIRTUAL MACHINES)
description: VRINFRGERPRNT04 is a dedicated Windows printserver VM identified in the document as an SAP Printserver that hosts SAP print queues and serves as a second example in a multi-server deployment. It was implicated in the same incident as VRINFRGERPRNT03: SAP printing via VRINFRGERPRNT04 failed, operators attempted a server restart and a print spooler restart, and the server was rolled back to its pre-maintenance state; during subsequent tests the machine experienced blue screens (BSODs). The identical failure pattern to VRINFRGERPRNT03 highlights a likely common, server-specific issue affecting the SAP printserver instances in this deployment.

title: PRINT QUEUES
description: Logical queues on the Windows Printservers that receive SAP print jobs. The document mandates standardized queue properties and consistency of sharing/publishing parameters; queue misconfiguration is a primary cause of SAP printing failures after updates/restores.

title: QUEUE PROPERTIES
description: The configuration attributes of a print queue (Queue Properties) including publishing/sharing settings and other parameters that must be standardized and validated after updates or VM restores to ensure proper SAP printing and spool processing.

title: PHYSICAL PRINTERS
description: The networked/attached printers that ultimately receive print output from Windows Printservers. Print queues map to these physical devices; reachability and network path to these printers are part of troubleshooting.

title: PRINT SPOOLER SERVICE
description: PRINT SPOOLER SERVICE: A Windows service running on the print server that manages the print queue and job spooling. During analysis the service was observed to be active, indicating the spooler itself was running while print jobs were not reaching the printer due to network reachability issues. Restarting the Print Spooler can temporarily change symptoms, but it may not correct underlying queue-level configuration deviations; the service is therefore included on the operational validation checklist and troubleshooting should cover both network reachability and queue/configuration checks.

title: NETWORK SHARING / PUBLISHING MECHANISMS
type: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE (FIREWALLS, VLANS, NAT, LOAD BALANCERS)
description: Mechanisms and network-level configuration used to share and publish print queues (SMB/print sharing, directory publishing, network visibility). The document highlights these settings as potentially impacted by updates/restores and important to validate for SAP print paths to function.

title: VM RESTORE
type: SERVER INFRASTRUCTURE (APPLICATION SERVERS, DOMAIN CONTROLLERS, VIRTUAL MACHINES)
description: Virtual machine restore procedures and events referenced in the document as a common trigger for incorrect queue/sharing state and subsequent printing issues; VM restore is treated as a change requiring post-restore validation of print infrastructure.

title: WINDOWS UPDATE EVENTS
description: Windows patch/update activities referenced as another common trigger for printing issues; the standard requires validation of print servers, queues, spooler and publishing after such updates.

title: SAP PRINT PATHS
description: The logical and network routes from SAP through SAP spool/output management to SAP print targets and onward to Windows print queues and physical printers. Classified in the document as business-critical and requiring priority handling when disrupted.

title: DECISION TREE & USE CASES
description: DECISION TREE & USE CASES: This deliverable is a consolidated, operational decision tree and set of use cases embedded in the blueprint and standard that guides operators and administrators through verification, diagnosis, and remediation of common federation, access, and printing problems. It combines a checklist-style troubleshooting flow (questions/checklist and use cases) and a five‑question diagnostic sequence to validate federation configuration, presence settings, recent external-access policy changes, and domain allow‑list entries; to determine whether an access issue stems from SharePoint’s permission model, folder-level permissions, direct membership, nested-group correctness, or historical/parallel groups; and to distinguish SAP‑only versus general printing failures. The document prescribes the sequence of checks (e.g., recent updates/restores, queue visibility, spooler state), test actions (test prints, membership/permission checks), and prescriptive remediation steps, thereby operationalizing the standard’s checks and remediation flows for consistent incident handling.

title: PUBLISHING/SHARING PARAMETERS
description: Configuration attributes that control whether and how print queues are published/shared (e.g., directory publishing, share flags and visibility). The document instructs validation and standardization of these parameters after updates or VM restores as they directly affect SAP → Windows print path visibility.

title: NETWORK PATH / PRINTER REACHABILITY
type: NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE (FIREWALLS, VLANS, NAT, LOAD BALANCERS)
description: Network-level connectivity between Windows Printservers and physical printers (including routing, firewall rules, VLAN reachability). The decision tree instructs checking network path/printer reachability when queues are visible but no output is produced.

title: SAP-SIDE ERRORS
description: Errors, logs and alerts generated by the SAP system (e.g., SAP Spool errors, print target mapping failures) that indicate issues on the SAP side rather than the Windows printservers; referenced as part of checks when all print servers are affected.

title: TEST PRINT (FROM SAP)
description: A validation action invoked by SAP to confirm end-to-end printing (sending a test print job from SAP to the printserver/queue/physical printer). The standard requires performing test prints as part of post-update/restore validation.

title: QUEUE STATUS
description: The operational state of a print queue (running/paused, jobs present, job states). The document prescribes checking queue status as a primary checkpoint after updates or VM restores and as part of the decision tree when troubleshooting print failures.

title: KF52: MICROSOFT TEAMS – INCOMING CALLS FROM SPECIFIC NUMBERS NOT RECEIVED (TI-1612036)
description: Support incident opened 18.09.25 reporting that incoming calls from certain customer numbers were not delivered. Ticket created via email, assigned to Employee 41, investigated with carrier and SBC log analysis, temporary tests performed, routing and translation rules adjusted per Direct Routing Blueprint, and closed 29.09.25 with confirmation that incoming calls were fully functional again.

title: CONTACT PERSON 22
description: Named contact at Customer Company 52 AG who reported the symptom (callers receive busy tone/disconnect), provided example numbers (+41 44 785 23 10, +41 44 785 23 11) and confirmed impact and timeline.

title: EMPLOYEE 41
description: Support engineer / ticket owner assigned to TI-1612036 who coordinated troubleshooting, performed internal analysis (SBC log review, tests), liaised with carrier and customer, and implemented adjustments to routing/translation rules per architecture to resolve the incident.

title: BLUEPRINT – MICROSOFT TEAMS DIRECT ROUTING & NUMBER TRANSLATION – VERSION 2.3
description: Internal architectural/operational document covering normalization of incoming numbers, E.164 formatting, translation rules on the SBC, voice routing policies and number translation procedures. Referenced during analysis and used as the specification for final configuration changes.

title: TEAMS ADMIN CENTER
description: TEAMS ADMIN CENTER is the Microsoft Teams administrative and diagnostic console used to manage and troubleshoot Teams telephony.
